name: Cyber Truck
info: I found a project called Crankshaft which can link an Android and Raspberry Pi to allow you to stream your phone's apps to the Raspberry Pi.  I 3D printed a housing for the Raspberry Pi and its screen to replace the stock radio console.  Wiring was a little painful due to trying to find an accurate wiring diagram for my Ford Ranger's year.  After it was sorted, I was able to boot the Pi when my truck started and navigate fairly seamlessly using Google Maps on my Android.  However, there were some major issues which I did not have the time to sort out.  Safely shutting down the Pi was an issue since when I turned off my truck, the power to the Pi would immediately be cut.  Also, the wiring harnesses I made to power the Pi and screen weren't the most secure causing the Pi to reboot if I ran over a large enough bump in the road.

name: RPi Arcade Cab
info: My makerspace had this arcade cabinet sitting around for years.  It was formerly a cabinet built for an open source project called Winitron.  At the time I came across the cabinet, it was just collecting dust.  I removed the old PC and CRT monitor, replacing them with a PC running Ubuntu and an LCD monitor.  Retro Pi was installed on the PC and set up to run on startup.  The controls were replaced with a 2 player setup to allow more button options for systems like SNES and N64.  While it was functional, with most of my side projects, time did not allow for work to continue.

name: Red Bear Head
short: Custom cabinet for a Red Bear tube amp
info: Red Bear was a small amp company who sold heads, cabs and studio amps in the late 1980s and early 1990s.  They were Russian made and distributed by Gibson.  I was lucky enough to stumble across owning a 30 watt studio amp made by them.  The amp is very well made and offers a warm tone with a nice crunchy fuzz when gain is applied.  One day, my cat completely tore up the fabric coating the amp and then the speaker cracked.  I didn't want to lose the head so I built a head cabinet for it and laser cut a panel.  After some finishing and wood stain, the head is still holding up great.

name: Celestion Cab
short: Custom cabinet based on Orange designs
info: My brother gifted two Celestion guitar speakers to me from a broken guitar cab of his.  The speakers were great and similar to what is used in Orange cabinets, which happen to be a favorite of mine.  I wanted to do the amps justice and build a proper cab.  Using layers of 3/4 inch thick mdf, wood glue and inspiration from Orange cab dimensions, I built an overengineered cabinet.

name: RPi CRTV Monitor
short: Using a CRTV as a Raspberry Pi Monitor
info: I picked up this portable TV from a garage sale and wanted to see if I could use it for a Raspberry Pi.  After finding the series of adapters and converters I needed to convert the HDMi output from the Pi to a UHF input on the CRT, I was able to connect the Pi to the CRT.  The black white color of the CRT makes for a great visual effect when playing videos.  Also, by adjusting the dials on the CRT, static effects can be made.

name: Guitars
info: After talking to a friend about wanting to build a guitar, he gifted me two low end guitars to experiment with. I replaced nearly all of the hardware, upgraded the pickups and added some stains to make some great sounding guitars.  I laser cut the pickguards and wired everything myself.  The bodies are still a little rough and the necks aren't the best but I think they sound good for what they are.

name: Genius Tunnel
info: The Genius Tunnel was a storefront display built for Moncler's two Hawaii locations.  Each of the series of tunnels used a Raspberry Pi to control hundreds of feet of RGB LEDs wired through each structure.  The LEDs were controlled using a daisy chain of LED DMX decoders connected to an eDMX Pro interface, which was then plugged into the Raspberry Pi's USB port.  A script written in the Processing IDE controlled the LED lighting sequences.

name: Moose Goldberg
short: A Rube Goldberg inspired dumb waiter
info: Moose Goldberg was a Rube Goldberg inspired two story dumb waiter designed and fabricated by ARCH Productions for the launch of Moose Knuckle's new store located in New York City, NY.  The machine included a functional dumb waiter to move coats between floors as well as numerous mechanical elements including a sequence of automated bobble heads, Newton's Cradle and a coat hanger which moved a coat around the structure on a custom designed track.  An Arduino Mega was used to control the many motors and sensors throughout the structure.  A script written in the Arduino IDE controlled each of the elements through a timed sequence of events.  A large red button was used as the start button for the events.  Certain elements required parts which could not be bought.  These were modeled in Fusion 360 and 3D printed using a Flashforge Creator Pro.

name: Open The Wound
short: A prototype for an art project
info: Working with Lane Shi Otayonii, I designed and built a prototype to work out the details of a new piece.  The wood frame would be mounted on the ceiling and open up to reveal a mounted TV playing video clips.  A Raspberry Pi was used to run a Python script to play the videos used in the piece.  An Arduino Nano was used to monitor the start/stop button and control the DC motors to open/close the frame.  Hall effect sensors were used for home and end-stop positions.  Custom gears, motor and sensor mounts were 3D printed and designed in Fusion 360.

name: CC Robot
info: Lane Shi Otayonii created CC Robot for her Automation Alive piece.  CC Robot uses a Teensy 4.0 to receive MIDI input from a score created in Ableton Live to control the various motors, sensors and LEDs mounted in CC.  Each arm uses a DC motor and two servo motors to make gestures such as hugging, embracing the crowd and touching its head.  A servo motor is used to move its mouth to allow CC to lip sync with audio tracks during the performance.  A WS2812 LED ring mounted inside the head illuminates the silicon mold mounted on top.  Brackets for mounting the plastic shells, silicon molds, LEDs, motors and other various components were modeled using Fusion 360 and 3D printed on a Flashforge Creator Pro.

name: Vial Dispenser
info: The Vial Dispenser was designed and built as a prototype for a new version of the Siren, a game I helped build for the Museum of Sex NYC.  The game involves a metal crank wheel cleverly placed for players to spin.  If the players can keep the wheel spinning for 45 seconds, they win a vial of her essence which is dispensed by the game.  A Raspberry Pi runs a sketch written in Python to control audio playback, game state changes, and serial communication with an Arduino Nano connected via USB.  The Nano runs a script written to receive and send data to the Pi as well as control the motors, sensor input and various lighting elements.  CAD models of the dispenser and parts were designed using Fusion 360.  When designs were finalized, parts were 3D printed, ordered from McMaster- Carr or built at my workshop.  Once assembled, testing took place over the course of many days to ensure all mechanisms were working before both dispensers were shipped to a fabrication shop who would incorporate my design into the final build.

name: Lucky Lips
info: Lucky Lips uses a GSR sensor to measure the strength of a kiss.  The players grab a hold of a metal hand rail mounted on opposite sides of the game and when they kiss or make skin-to-skin contact, the GSR sensor measures the conductance of the two players.  If the players can maintain contact throughout the game, they win a random prize from the wheel mounted in the center.  While most prizes are tongue in cheek suggestions, grand prizes such as free hotel rooms or drink tickets were offered.  An Arduino Uno was used to read sensor data and send it to a Raspberry Pi.  The Pi ran a Processing IDE sketch to control game state changes, color patterns for the multiple sections of LED strips, and handle sensor input from the Arduino Uno using the Firmata protocol.  A Python sketch controlled all audio playback, printing of prize tickets, and emailing staff to alert them when the grand prizes were won. The two sketches communicated locally via the Open Sound Protocol.

name: Glory Stall
info: The Glory Stall is a hilarious take on the Wack-A-Mole game featured in many carnivals and arcades across the country.  Up to 4 players can compete to see who can pull the most.  The player who can 'yank the most planks' wins their choice of a stuffed pepper, eggplant or banana.  Each stall uses a combination of an Arduino Nano and Raspberry Pi to control the various sensors, hardware, and audio playback in each stall.  Each of the 4 Raspberry Pis act as clients and connect to a Raspberry Pi acting as the game's server using ethernet cables and a desktop switch.  The server Pi awaits user input from the staff to start and stop the game.  The Pis communicate using Open Sound Control.  A Processing IDE sketch is used to control each client and server Pi.  The Processing sketch handles audio playback, game state changes, scoreboard updating and communication with the Arduino Nano controlling sensors and hardware via the Firmata protocol.
